bins.name: Joint and Accurate Interconnection Study
bins.desc: ClearPath supports jointly studying large load and supply-side interconnection customers to reduce speculative queue behavior, identify cost-effective transmission solutions, and ensure all beneficiaries pay. It also recommends that studies use actual operating characteristics rather than worst-case scenarios, which overstate impacts and inflate costs for flexible loads.
quotes: ClearPath strongly supports the ANOPR's proposal to jointly study supply-side interconnection customers and large load interconnection customers to the extent practicable.

developers of both generation and large loads often submit multiple interconnection requests as a means of price discovery and time-to-grid discovery. This creates unmanageable queue volumes, inflated demand and supply forecasts, and significant delays to the interconnection process due to projects withdrawing late into the process when they discover their project is not economically feasible due to costs or energization timelines.

Coordinating interconnection studies for large loads with supply-side interconnection can enable the identification of more cost-effective transmission solutions and ensure that all benefiting customers pay, whether they are load or supply customers.

ClearPath recommends that FERC require transmission providers to study large loads based on their known operating characteristics to increase the accuracy of these studies, avoid unnecessary network upgrades that can slow interconnection, and reduce excessive costs.

We recommend that FERC adopt a similar approach for studying large load withdrawals and/or injections. This advances just and reasonable rates by avoiding overestimation of the impacts of large loads through study assumptions that depict worst-case scenarios, such as curtailable large loads withdrawing at full capacity during peak demand periods

bins.name: Coordinated Load and Supply Study
bins.desc: ClearPath endorses joint processing of large load and generation interconnection requests to align commercial timelines for offtake agreements and to enable identification of shared transmission solutions. This approach ensures that all customers benefiting from a transmission upgrade contribute to its cost.
quotes: ClearPath strongly supports the ANOPR's proposal to jointly study supply-side interconnection customers and large load interconnection customers to the extent practicable.

Coordinating interconnection studies for large loads with supply-side interconnection can enable the identification of more cost-effective transmission solutions and ensure that all benefiting customers pay, whether they are load or supply customers.

bins.name: Flexible Operations and Market Access
bins.desc: ClearPath argues that study assumptions and interconnection agreements must reflect the dispatchable nature of large loads such as thermal energy storage, electrolyzers, and electric arc furnaces. Worst-case study assumptions impose unnecessary upgrade costs on flexible loads, and interconnection agreements must not bar these loads from providing ancillary services or wholesale market participation.
quotes: ClearPath recommends that FERC require transmission providers to study large loads based on their known operating characteristics to increase the accuracy of these studies, avoid unnecessary network upgrades that can slow interconnection, and reduce excessive costs.

We recommend that FERC adopt a similar approach for studying large load withdrawals and/or injections. This advances just and reasonable rates by avoiding overestimation of the impacts of large loads through study assumptions that depict worst-case scenarios, such as curtailable large loads withdrawing at full capacity during peak demand periods

Any interconnection agreement for large load cannot prohibit the load from engaging in jurisdictional wholesale market activities such as the provision of energy (e.g., discharge of energy from storage into the grid) or ancillary services, including voltage support and other similar support for balancing the grid.
FERC must ensure that dispatchable large loads, in addition to co-located generating facilities, are fully compensated for providing energy and ancillary services to the grid.

Cost-allocation of network upgrades that combine volumetric and coincident peak demand charges can incentivize flexible large loads to avoid system peaks, therefore reducing system impacts and the need for additional infrastructure investments.
This regulatory framework incentivized EAFs to curtail load during critical peak demand and shift production, leading to a reduction of peak loads by 91%.

bins.name: Co-located Facility Compensation
bins.desc: ClearPath calls on FERC to ensure that dispatchable large loads and their co-located generating facilities are fully compensated for energy and ancillary services provided to the grid.
quotes: FERC must ensure that dispatchable large loads, in addition to co-located generating facilities, are fully compensated for providing energy and ancillary services to the grid.

bins.name: Cost Allocation Aligned with System Use
bins.desc: ClearPath recommends that FERC set guardrails preventing cost-shifting and free riding without prescribing a single methodology, so that utilities can experiment with rate designs. It favors combining volumetric and coincident peak demand charges to incentivize load shifting and cites a critical peak pricing pilot where electric arc furnaces reduced peak loads by 91%.
quotes: developers of both generation and large loads often submit multiple interconnection requests as a means of price discovery and time-to-grid discovery. This creates unmanageable queue volumes, inflated demand and supply forecasts, and significant delays to the interconnection process due to projects withdrawing late into the process when they discover their project is not economically feasible due to costs or energization timelines.

Coordinating interconnection studies for large loads with supply-side interconnection can enable the identification of more cost-effective transmission solutions and ensure that all benefiting customers pay, whether they are load or supply customers.

We recommend that FERC adopt a similar approach for studying large load withdrawals and/or injections. This advances just and reasonable rates by avoiding overestimation of the impacts of large loads through study assumptions that depict worst-case scenarios, such as curtailable large loads withdrawing at full capacity during peak demand periods

ClearPath recommends that FERC establish clear guardrails for how utilities serving large loads allocate costs for transmission and ancillary services to prevent cost-shifting or free riding, rather than prescribing a particular methodology.

Cost-allocation of network upgrades that combine volumetric and coincident peak demand charges can incentivize flexible large loads to avoid system peaks, therefore reducing system impacts and the need for additional infrastructure investments.
This regulatory framework incentivized EAFs to curtail load during critical peak demand and shift production, leading to a reduction of peak loads by 91%.

bins.name: FERC Jurisdiction Over Wholesale Access
bins.desc: ClearPath contends that FERC's authority under FPA sections 201 and 205 extends to wholesale market participation by dispatchable large loads, including energy sales and ancillary services. It argues that no current regulatory framework grants these loads access to wholesale energy purchase prices and urges FERC to address this gap alongside interconnection reforms.
quotes: Any interconnection agreement for large load cannot prohibit the load from engaging in jurisdictional wholesale market activities such as the provision of energy (e.g., discharge of energy from storage into the grid) or ancillary services, including voltage support and other similar support for balancing the grid.
FERC must ensure that dispatchable large loads, in addition to co-located generating facilities, are fully compensated for providing energy and ancillary services to the grid.
there is no regulatory framework that provides dispatchable large loads access to purchasing energy at wholesale energy prices.

Simply, adopting large load interconnection procedures alone is not enough. We urge the Commission to take into consideration how its large load interconnection procedures and other FERC initiatives can ensure accessibility of large load access to wholesale energy markets.

ClearPath recommends that FERC establish clear guardrails for how utilities serving large loads allocate costs for transmission and ancillary services to prevent cost-shifting or free riding, rather than prescribing a particular methodology.

bins.name: Wholesale Energy Purchase Access Gap
bins.desc: ClearPath identifies a structural gap: dispatchable large loads cannot purchase electricity at wholesale prices under Orders 745, 841, or 2222, leaving them at an economic disadvantage relative to storage resources. The filing urges FERC to pair interconnection reforms with action on wholesale market access, pointing to a South Dakota ESA with a wholesale-linked pricing rider as a state-level example.
quotes: Any interconnection agreement for large load cannot prohibit the load from engaging in jurisdictional wholesale market activities such as the provision of energy (e.g., discharge of energy from storage into the grid) or ancillary services, including voltage support and other similar support for balancing the grid.
FERC must ensure that dispatchable large loads, in addition to co-located generating facilities, are fully compensated for providing energy and ancillary services to the grid.
there is no regulatory framework that provides dispatchable large loads access to purchasing energy at wholesale energy prices.

Simply, adopting large load interconnection procedures alone is not enough. We urge the Commission to take into consideration how its large load interconnection procedures and other FERC initiatives can ensure accessibility of large load access to wholesale energy markets.

bins.name: Dispatchable Load Technology Evidence
bins.desc: ClearPath provides concrete examples -- thermal energy storage, electrolyzers, and electric arc furnaces -- to illustrate how large loads can follow price signals, provide ancillary services, and reduce peak demand. It cites the 91% peak load reduction achieved by electric arc furnaces under a critical peak pricing pilot as evidence that flexible rate structures produce significant system benefits.
quotes: This regulatory framework incentivized EAFs to curtail load during critical peak demand and shift production, leading to a reduction of peak loads by 91%.
